learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate the ways in which performance texts are adapted to engage intended or specific audiences.
Identify generic, structural, temporal, spatial, bodied and linguistic features of texts in performance.
Comprehend the importance of the elements of performance in practice through group performance or backstage secondment.
Have an awareness of the historical context and dramaturgical sensibility of a range of plays.
Begin to develop an understanding of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander knowledges and texts and cultivate an appreciation of how cultural competencies apply to the performing arts industry.
Practice independent learning, and developing applied industry skills through participating in a student theatre festival.

assessments.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessments.task_description:
Task overview — Tutorial Activities

This task assesses your engagement with the course across the Semester. In weeks 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 9, your engagement with and participation in the course content in tutorial activities is worth a total of 30%. This task assesses your activities in tutorials across the Semester across two main areas; meaningful participation and meaningful engagement. A detailed assignment brief and rubric will be available on BlackBoard.

Task Description

The three components of meaningful participation assessed in tutorials are: volunteering for activities, respectful peer collaboration and engaging with the ideas of others. Participation means willing involvement with the tasks at hand and each other, asking questions, working well in groups, etc., however acting ability is not assessed. Meaningful participation is worth 3/5 of your grade. The two components of meaningful engagement assessed are: reading of the text/s and absorption of lecture content. You will demonstrate your engagement through the application of ideas in discussion, practical activities and in short (100 word), handwritten reflections where you have 10 minutes to complete the provided task. Meaningful engagement is worth 2/5 of your grade.

There are 7 opportunities across the semester, each worth 5%. Only your best 6 results count, together forming 30% of your final grade. Because only 6 of the 7 count, the assessment already builds in the flexibility to miss up to one session — for illness or any other reason — with no penalty and no documentation required. Your marks and comments will be released in 3 tranches across the semester in weeks 3, 6 and 9.

The written task part of meaningful engagement is handwritten and closed-book: no written material, electronic material or electronic devices may be used. All such items must remain in your bag, and all electronic devices must be switched off or in airplane mode. If a device emits any noise, you may be subject to an allegation of misconduct and the item may be confiscated for the duration of the exercise.

There are no opportunities to re-take tutorial activities, and no alternative assessment is available except in the specific circumstances set out below. As this is an internal course, you must make yourself available at the scheduled date, time and venue.

Alternative assessment and accommodations

If you have a Disability Action Plan relevant to this task, please contact the course coordinator as early as possible. Reasonable accommodations can be made for matters documented in a Disability Action Plan (for example, if you are unable to handwrite under timed conditions).

For the opportunity to be graded at a pass (in the range of a four) or above against the meaningful engagement criteria, a separate written make-up session is available only to students who, for a genuine and documented reason, miss more than 1 tutorial— for example, a sudden or serious illness, or another documented cause requiring a lengthy absence.

To be considered for a make-up session or any other alternative arrangement, you must contact the course coordinator with your documentation as soon as possible after the circumstances arise. One make-up session for the meaningful engagement parts of the tutorial activities will be offered in the Week 10 lecture slot; if you are eligible, please ensure you are available.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This is an in-person assessment completed during normal tutorial hours.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date:
18/09/2026 4:00 pm

Students will be asked to attend a live performance from a short list and write a critical review.
assessments.task_description:
Students will attend a live performance, and will write a critical performance review reflecting on how TWO elements of performance (genre, structure, time, space, body, and/or language) manifest in, and influence your experience of the performance. 

To complete this task, you must arrange to see ONE of the following Brisbane Festival shows:

·        La Boite’s production of Koreaboo! by Michelle Lim Davidson, directed by Jessica Arthur.

·        The Sydney Theatre Company production of Strong is the New Pretty, by Suzie Miller, directed by Sarah Giles.

·        The Farm’s devised production of No One Gets Out of Here Alive.

A fully detailed assignment brief will be made available via Blackboard early in the semester. Please note that students are responsible for buying their own tickets, and attending the production independently of the course timetable.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students will submit this assessment via Turnitin on Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
29/10/2026 - 31/10/2026

This assessment will take place in person, during Vanguard Theatre Festival at the Avalon Theatre in Week 13. You will be placed on one of three pathways: performance, devising, or backstage secondment.
assessments.task_description:
This task is designed to allow you to demonstrate your understanding of the elements of performance through practice. You do this through one of three ways working on the Vanguard festival of student work in Week 13: either as an actor, working under the guidance of a DRAM3105 student director to realise a performance in a short play; as a performer, devising a short performance in collaboration with a group of your DRAM1010 peers; or via a secondment to the backstage crew.

Detailed assignment briefs for each path will be provided via Blackboard. 

Notes about group work

If you are placed in a group, group members will initially receive the same grade out of 40 for the performance. A peer factor will then be applied to your mark to recognise the work you do in your group, and your mark may go up or down. This will be determined through a peer-accountability survey, where you will be required to provide feedback on the work of your peers against criteria in BuddyCheck. If you do not complete the peer assessment survey, you will not be eligible for additional marks, and your mark may go down. If you are placed in the performance pathway, then feedback from your director about your group work may be taken into consideration when determining your peer factor.

If you are placed on the secondment pathway, then each individual will receive a grade out of 40 for the assessment. Your ability to work as part of a team will be assessed as part of the criteria for the secondment pathway.

Notes about flexibility and time commitment:

Rehearsals:

The bulk of rehearsals will be undertaken in tutorials in Weeks 10, 11 and 12. After the mid-semester break, performance and devising pathway students' timetables will change, and we will give advanced warning of this. We will not schedule your new tutorial when you have another class. Secondment pathway students are not required to attend tutorials in weeks 10 -12.

Plotting and Technical rehearsals

Plotting may occur across Monday 19 October to Saturday 24 October, primarily in afternoons and evenings, with some daytime work possible on Friday 23 October and Saturday 24 October. Technical rehearsals may occur across Saturday 24 October to Thursday 29 October, with afternoon and evening work expected between 25 and 28 October. In the event of unforeseen technical issues or other emergencies, daytime technical rehearsals may be required between Thursday 29 October and Saturday 31 October.

Secondment pathway students will be required for the majority of the rehearsals. Devising pathway students will be required for their scheduled plotting session, and devising pathway and performance pathway students will be required for their scheduled technical rehearsal. The schedule will be released after the mid-semester break, and we will take into account your other classes.

Performance

Each group will be allocated one performance night on Thursday 29/10, Friday 30/10 or Saturday 31/10 (Week 13). You will be required from 3 pm on your performance evening. However, we will not schedule your performance when you have other university classes. You are also required to assist on front of house and attend as an audience member. You are required to stay back after your performance and assist with the bump-out.

Students should keep their commitments outside the University as flexible as possible during the festival period (weeks 12 and 13), as performance dates and times may require adjustment before the final schedule is confirmed.

Recording of Oral and Practical Assessment

  • All presentations will be recorded for marking purposes via recording facilities available where the assessment takes place (eg. ECHO360, Zoom, camera device)
  • Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course.
  • Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of:
  • Moderation of marking;
  • Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or
  • Re-marking following a successful re-mark application.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students will be required to attend an audition in week 8, this counts towards their Vanguard assessment mark.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1: Introduction - Elements of Performance

This 90-minute lecture will introduce the six key elements of performance covered in the course, as well as familiarise students with the assessment regime and other course requirements.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 1: Introduction - Elements of Performance This 90-minute lecture will introduce the six key elements of performance covered in the course, as well as familiarise students with the assessment regime and other course requirements.

learning_activities.topic: Week 1: NO TUTORIALS
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 1: NO TUTORIALS

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2: Genre - Agamemnon

In the 90-minute week two lecture, the first element, genre, will be introduced with reference to the historical division between tragedy and comedy, dating from Ancient Greek theatre.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 2: Genre - Agamemnon In the 90-minute week two lecture, the first element, genre, will be introduced with reference to the historical division between tragedy and comedy, dating from Ancient Greek theatre.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2: Staging Genre

In this tutorial, students will experiment with the text of "Agamemnon" to understand how genre has been staged across time, with specific reference to formal devices from Greek tragedy.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 2: Staging Genre In this tutorial, students will experiment with the text of "Agamemnon" to understand how genre has been staged across time, with specific reference to formal devices from Greek tragedy.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 3: Structure - Love and Information

This 90-minute lecture will introduce students to the basic elements of structure, with particular reference to the plot/narrative divide (that is, how can similar stories be told in different ways?).
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 3: Structure - Love and Information This 90-minute lecture will introduce students to the basic elements of structure, with particular reference to the plot/narrative divide (that is, how can similar stories be told in different ways?).

learning_activities.topic:
Week 3: Building Structure

This tutorial will use selected scenes from "Love & Information" in order to experiment with building structure in performance.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 3: Building Structure This tutorial will use selected scenes from "Love & Information" in order to experiment with building structure in performance.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4: Time - Indecent

This 90-minute lecture will consider the ways in which playwrights use time to create dramatic meaning, and how time can be related to (and enact) structure.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 4: Time - Indecent This 90-minute lecture will consider the ways in which playwrights use time to create dramatic meaning, and how time can be related to (and enact) structure.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4: Marking Time

In this tutorial, we will experiment with different techniques of marking time in performance, with specific reference to the distinction between 'real' time and dramatic time in "Indecent" by Paula Vogel.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 4: Marking Time In this tutorial, we will experiment with different techniques of marking time in performance, with specific reference to the distinction between 'real' time and dramatic time in "Indecent" by Paula Vogel.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5: Space - Picnic at Hanging Rock

This 90-minute lecture considers the real and imagined spaces and places in which performance takes place, using the case study of contemporary Australian plays.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 5: Space - Picnic at Hanging Rock This 90-minute lecture considers the real and imagined spaces and places in which performance takes place, using the case study of contemporary Australian plays.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5: Playing in Space

This tutorial will experiment with the many and varied uses of space in performance, and consider in particular how space can be used to complicate the effects of time and structure in "Picnic at Hanging Rock".
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 5: Playing in Space This tutorial will experiment with the many and varied uses of space in performance, and consider in particular how space can be used to complicate the effects of time and structure in "Picnic at Hanging Rock".

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6: Body - Wise Children

This 90-minute lecture will examine the central role of the body in performance, using a physical theatre case study to explore how the body communicates meaning in performance.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 6: Body - Wise Children This 90-minute lecture will examine the central role of the body in performance, using a physical theatre case study to explore how the body communicates meaning in performance.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6: Bending Bodies

This tutorial will examine the different ways that bodies communicate meaning from a selected physical theatre performance in order to explore how the body might be deployed meaningfully in performance, and especially how the body relates to space.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 6: Bending Bodies This tutorial will examine the different ways that bodies communicate meaning from a selected physical theatre performance in order to explore how the body might be deployed meaningfully in performance, and especially how the body relates to space.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7: Language - Othello

Analysing the tri-lingual nature of Jimi Bani and Jason Klarwein's adaptation of William Shakespeare's "Othello" this 90-minute lecture will examine how language enacts and constitutes performance.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 7: Language - Othello Analysing the tri-lingual nature of Jimi Bani and Jason Klarwein's adaptation of William Shakespeare's "Othello" this 90-minute lecture will examine how language enacts and constitutes performance.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7: Language at Work

This tutorial will examine the ways in which language can create character and help shape genre using scenes from Bani and Klarwin's adaptation of "Othello"
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 7: Language at Work This tutorial will examine the ways in which language can create character and help shape genre using scenes from Bani and Klarwin's adaptation of "Othello"

learning_activities.topic: Week 8: NO LECTURE
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 8: NO LECTURE

learning_activities.topic:
Week 8: AUDITIONS

Tutorials will be given over to auditions, where the DRAM3105 directing students will consider which students they would like to work with for the Performance Pathway collaboration. collaboration.

Students may be asked to attend a tutorial at a different time, and all students are asked to participate in the audition process.

Learning outcomes: L03, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 8: AUDITIONS Tutorials will be given over to auditions, where the DRAM3105 directing students will consider which students they would like to work with for the Performance Pathway collaboration. collaboration. Students may be asked to attend a tutorial at a different time, and all students are asked to participate in the audition process.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 9: Elements of Performance - Hedda Gabler

This final 90-minute lecture will offer a case study of the elements of performance working together in the live theatre experience. It will also introduce students to the UQ Drama program.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 9: Elements of Performance - Hedda Gabler This final 90-minute lecture will offer a case study of the elements of performance working together in the live theatre experience. It will also introduce students to the UQ Drama program.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 9: The elements at work

This tutorial will explore the relationship between the six elements of performance using "Hedda Gabler" as an example.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 9: The elements at work This tutorial will explore the relationship between the six elements of performance using "Hedda Gabler" as an example.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12: Vanguard Plotting and Technical Rehearsals

In preparation for the delivery of the Vanguard Festival. Plotting may occur across Monday 19 October to Saturday 24 October, primarily in afternoons and evenings, with some daytime work possible on Friday 23 and Saturday 24 October. Students on the devising pathway are required to attend their scheduled one-hour plotting session.

Technical rehearsals may occur across Saturday 24 October to Thursday 29 October, with afternoon and evening work expected between 26 and 28 October. In the event of unforeseen technical issues or other emergencies, daytime technical rehearsals may be required between Thursday 29 October and Saturday 31 October. Devising and performance pathway students are required to attend their scheduled one-hour technical rehearsal.

Students on the Secondment pathway will be required at various times from Monday 19 to Saturday 24 October.

A schedule will be provided closer to the date, and students should keep their commitments outside the University as flexible as possible during the festival period, as performance dates and times may require adjustment before the final schedule is confirmed.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 13: Vanguard Performances

The Vanguard Festival will be delivered across three evenings of performance, from Thursday 29 October through Saturday 31 October, with dress rehearsals running in the afternoon of the performance. Performance and Devising pathway students will be required to attend between 3pm and 10pm on their scheduled performance day.

They are also required to attend as an audience member and assist in front of house on one other night, and assist with bump out each evening after the performance.

Secondment pathway students will be required for multiple afternoons and evenings across the festival.

A schedule will be provided closer to the date, and students should keep their commitments outside the University as flexible as possible during the festival period, as performance dates and times may require adjustment before the final schedule is confirmed.
